items.correct: A sperm nucleus fuses with an egg nucleus to form a diploid zygote, combining genetic material from both parents.
items.misconception: Fertilisation is just the sperm reaching the egg's surface.
items.random_wrong: Fertilisation is the release of an egg from the ovary.
items.why: Students confuse fertilisation with ovulation or with the sperm merely arriving at the egg.

items.q: What does FSH do in the menstrual cycle?
items.correct: FSH from the pituitary gland stimulates an egg to mature in the ovary and triggers oestrogen production.
items.misconception: FSH causes the uterus lining to break down.
items.random_wrong: FSH is produced by the ovary rather than the pituitary.
items.why: Students mix up the roles and sources of the menstrual cycle hormones.

items.q: Do children of the same parents have identical DNA?
items.correct: No - gametes vary because of meiosis and random fertilisation, so siblings (except identical twins) differ genetically.
items.misconception: Children of the same parents have identical DNA.
items.random_wrong: Children inherit DNA only from the mother.
items.why: Students overlook the genetic variation produced by meiosis and fertilisation.

items.q: Fertilisation in humans is the fusion of:
items.correct: a sperm nucleus and an egg nucleus
items.why: The answer examiners want is: a sperm nucleus and an egg nucleus. “two egg cells” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Which hormone stimulates an egg to mature in the ovary?
items.why: Correct answer: FSH. “progesterone” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.correct: release of an egg from the ovary
items.why: It's release of an egg from the ovary. “fusion of sperm and egg” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.correct: fertilising eggs with sperm outside the body
items.random_wrong: preventing the egg from being released
items.why: It's fertilising eggs with sperm outside the body. “cloning an adult cell” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
